subtopic: Heavy menstrual bleeding - investigation in a perimenopausal woman
stem: A 49-year-old G3P3 presents with a 9-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Cycles have shortened from 28 to 22 days, last 8 to 10 days, and she changes a fully soaked pad every 90 minutes on the worst two days with passage of 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She reports two episodes of intermenstrual bleeding in the last 3 months. Her BMI is 34, she has type 2 diabetes on metformin, and she has never been on hormonal contraception. There is no postcoital bleeding, no dyspareunia, and her last cervical screen 18 months ago was HPV negative. She is not sexually active currently and uses no contraception. On examination her abdomen is soft with no palpable mass; speculum shows a healthy cervix and no cervical lesion; bimanual reveals a bulky but mobile uterus, no adnexal mass. Pregnancy test is negative. FBC shows Hb 98 g/L with MCV 74 fL and ferritin 6 microg/L. TSH and coagulation screen are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/84 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: BMI 34. Bulky mobile uterus. Cervix healthy. No adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 74 fL, ferritin 6 microg/L, TSH normal, coags normal, HPV negative 18 months ago.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Commence tranexamic acid 1 g tds during menses and review in 3 months
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is first-line medical therapy for HMB without structural cause, but starting empirical treatment in a woman over 45 with IMB and high-risk features for endometrial cancer without first excluding malignancy is inappropriate (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test and HPV partial genotype now
options.rationale: She is in the routine 5-yearly screening interval and her HPV test was negative 18 months ago; the bleeding pattern points to an endometrial rather than cervical source (NCSP; RANZCOG).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to characterise the bulky uterus before further management
options.rationale: MRI is a second-line imaging modality used to map fibroids prior to surgery or when TVUS is inconclusive; it is not the appropriate first investigation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound and endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Age over 45 with HMB plus intermenstrual bleeding and obesity/diabetes (unopposed oestrogen risk factors) mandates endometrial assessment to exclude hyperplasia/carcinoma; TVUS plus Pipelle biopsy is the recommended first-line workup (RANZCOG AUB statement; Therapeutic Guidelines Gynaecology).

options.text: Outpatient hysteroscopy without prior endometrial sampling or imaging
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is indicated when TVUS shows focal pathology or when blind sampling is non-diagnostic; initial workup is TVUS plus Pipelle (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

explanation.summary: In HMB, age over 45, intermenstrual bleeding, persistent symptoms despite treatment, or risk factors for unopposed oestrogen exposure (obesity, diabetes, PCOS, tamoxifen, nulliparity, late menopause) require endometrial assessment before empirical medical therapy. TVUS measures endometrial thickness and identifies structural PALM lesions; Pipelle biopsy samples the endometrium for hyperplasia/carcinoma.
key_points: PALM (structural): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia
COEIN (non-structural): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
Endometrial sampling indicated in HMB >45, IMB, failed medical Rx, or risk factors
Management ladder if benign: tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, LNG-IUS, COCP, oral progestogen, ablation, hysterectomy
LNG-IUS is first-line long-term medical therapy for HMB without structural cause
explanation.pearls: A bulky uterus on bimanual examination in a perimenopausal woman with IMB is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise, regardless of how plausible adenomyosis or fibroids seem.

subtopic: Dysmenorrhoea - primary vs secondary differentiation and first-line therapy
stem: An 18-year-old nulligravid woman presents with painful periods since menarche at age 12. Cycles are regular at 30 days, last 5 days, and the pain is crampy suprapubic, starts within 4 hours of bleeding onset, peaks in the first 24 hours, and resolves by day 3. Pain is associated with nausea but no fever, no dyspareunia, no dyschezia, no cyclical haematuria, and no intermenstrual bleeding. She is sexually active with consistent condom use, has had one partner, has never had an STI screen, and has no family history of endometriosis. She has tried paracetamol 1 g qid with minimal relief. On examination she is well, BMI 22, abdomen soft and non-tender, and she declines vaginal examination. Pregnancy test is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Declines internal examination.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Pelvic ultrasound and refer to gynaecology for diagnostic laparoscopy
options.rationale: The history is typical of primary dysmenorrhoea (onset within 6-12 months of menarche, pain limited to menses, no secondary features); imaging and laparoscopy are reserved for treatment failure or red flags for secondary dysmenorrhoea (Jean Hailes; RANZCOG).

options.text: Trial naproxen 500 mg bd starting 24 hours before expected menses for 3 cycles
options.rationale: First-line treatment for primary dysmenorrhoea is an NSAID timed to start just before or at the onset of bleeding and continued for 2 to 3 days; naproxen has good evidence in adolescents and is recommended by Therapeutic Guidelines and Jean Hailes.

options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill as first-line therapy
options.rationale: COCP is second-line for primary dysmenorrhoea or first-line when contraception is also desired; NSAIDs are tried first when there is no contraceptive need (Therapeutic Guidelines; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is reserved for treatment-resistant dysmenorrhoea, adenomyosis or heavy bleeding; not first-line in an adolescent with classic primary dysmenorrhoea who has not yet had an NSAID trial (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Refer for cognitive behavioural therapy and pelvic floor physiotherapy
options.rationale: Multidisciplinary pain management has a role in chronic pelvic pain that has failed first-line medical therapy; it is not an appropriate first step for untreated primary dysmenorrhoea (RANZCOG Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: Primary dysmenorrhoea begins within 6 to 12 months of menarche, is confined to menses, and lacks secondary features. Secondary dysmenorrhoea presents later, has acyclic features, dyspareunia, dyschezia, cyclical haematuria, infertility, or pelvic mass. First-line for primary dysmenorrhoea is NSAID timed to menses; second-line is COCP. LNG-IUS is preferred for adenomyosis. Laparoscopy is gold standard for diagnosing endometriosis when medical therapy fails.
key_points: Primary dysmenorrhoea: prostaglandin-mediated, treat with NSAID first
Secondary dysmenorrhoea red flags: late onset, dyspareunia, dyschezia, IMB, infertility
NSAID timing: start 24 hours before expected menses, continue 2 to 3 days
COCP second-line or first-line if contraception desired
LNG-IUS first-line for adenomyosis-related dysmenorrhoea
explanation.pearls: Pain that started years after menarche and has cycle-independent features is not primary dysmenorrhoea, even if it gets worse with periods.

subtopic: Primary amenorrhoea with absent breast development - workup pathway
stem: A 16-year-old presents with primary amenorrhoea. She has never had a menstrual period. Her height is 148 cm (below 3rd centile), weight 48 kg, BMI 22. On Tanner staging her breast development is Tanner 1 and pubic hair is Tanner 3. She has a wide carrying angle at the elbows, a short fourth metacarpal, and a low posterior hairline. There is no anosmia and no visual field defect. She reports no cyclical pelvic pain, no abdominal mass, and no headaches. Her past medical history includes recurrent otitis media in childhood requiring grommets and a known bicuspid aortic valve picked up incidentally at age 8 on a school murmur referral. She has no sisters; her mother went through menarche at 13 and is 165 cm tall. There is no family history of consanguinity, autoimmune disease, or premature ovarian insufficiency. On examination her external genitalia are prepubertal Tanner stage; the vaginal opening is patent and a normal-appearing cervix is visible on careful inspection. Pelvic ultrasound shows a small infantile uterus and streak ovaries bilaterally with no follicular activity. A renal ultrasound performed in childhood was normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Height 148 cm (<3rd centile). Breast Tanner 1. Wide carrying angle. Short 4th metacarpal. Low posterior hairline.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS abdo: small uterus, streak ovaries bilaterally. External genitalia normal female.

lead_in: Which is the single most useful next investigation to confirm the underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Karyotype
options.rationale: Short stature with somatic features (wide carrying angle, short fourth metacarpal, low posterior hairline, bicuspid aortic valve), streak ovaries and absent breast development is Turner syndrome (45,XO); karyotype confirms the diagnosis and detects mosaicism (RANZCOG; ESHRE Turner 2017).

options.text: Serum prolactin
options.rationale: Hyperprolactinaemia typically causes secondary amenorrhoea with normal breast development and galactorrhoea, not primary amenorrhoea with the dysmorphic features described (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to characterise the uterus and adnexa
options.rationale: Ultrasound has already demonstrated streak ovaries and a small uterus; MRI is reserved for suspected Mullerian anomalies when ultrasound is equivocal (RANZCOG).

options.text: GnRH stimulation test
options.rationale: GnRH testing differentiates pituitary from hypothalamic causes of hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism; the streak ovaries and dysmorphic features point to a primary (gonadal) cause that karyotype will diagnose definitively (ESHRE).

options.text: Olfactory testing for anosmia
options.rationale: Anosmia raises Kallmann syndrome, which causes hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism with normal ovaries and uterus; the streak ovaries and short stature with somatic features are not features of Kallmann (StatPearls).

explanation.summary: Primary amenorrhoea is investigated by stratifying on breast development (oestrogen exposure) and presence of uterus. Absent breasts with elevated FSH and streak ovaries indicates gonadal dysgenesis (Turner 45,XO most common). Absent breasts with low FSH indicates hypothalamic-pituitary cause (Kallmann if anosmia). Normal breasts with absent uterus suggests Mullerian agenesis (MRKH) or androgen insensitivity (46,XY). Imperforate hymen presents with cyclical pain and a bulging bluish membrane.
key_points: Turner 45,XO: short stature, streak ovaries, bicuspid aortic valve, renal anomalies, lymphoedema in infancy
Kallmann: anosmia + hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism
MRKH: 46,XX, normal ovaries, absent/rudimentary uterus, normal breast development
Complete androgen insensitivity: 46,XY, female phenotype, blind-ending vagina, intra-abdominal testes
Imperforate hymen: cyclical pelvic pain, primary amenorrhoea, bluish bulging hymen
explanation.pearls: Short stature plus primary amenorrhoea is Turner syndrome until karyotype proves otherwise; remember to screen for aortic root dilatation and renal anomalies in every Turner patient.

subtopic: Secondary amenorrhoea in an athlete - hypothalamic differential
stem: A 24-year-old marathon runner presents with 11 months of amenorrhoea. Her menarche was at 13 and cycles were regular at 28 days until she increased training to 90 km per week 18 months ago in preparation for an ultramarathon. Her BMI has dropped from 22 to 18.5 in the last 12 months and she reports restricting carbohydrates and skipping meals on training days. She works as a primary school teacher and has noticed fatigue, low mood and cold intolerance for the last 4 months. She has no galactorrhoea, no hirsutism, no acne, no hot flushes, no vaginal dryness, no headaches, no visual disturbance, no recent dilatation and curettage or termination of pregnancy, and no history of pelvic infection. She has had no live births and uses condoms with her long-term male partner. A pregnancy test is negative. Her FSH is 2.8 IU/L, LH 1.6 IU/L, oestradiol 110 pmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, AMH 22 pmol/L, total testosterone 0.6 nmol/L. A bone density scan shows a Z-score of -2.3 at the lumbar spine and -1.8 at the femoral neck.

data_table.Examination: Thin, well. No acne or hirsutism. Normal external genitalia.
data_table.Investigations: FSH 2.8 IU/L, LH 1.6 IU/L, E2 110 pmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, AMH 22 pmol/L. DXA Z-score -2.3 lumbar spine. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Polycystic ovary syndrome
options.rationale: PCOS requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria (oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology); she has no hyperandrogenism and her LH is low rather than the elevated LH:FSH typical of PCOS (CREPCOS 2023; Monash International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea
options.rationale: Low FSH, low LH and low oestradiol in the setting of low energy availability (high training volume, restrictive eating, weight loss to BMI 18.5) and low bone density is classic functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea, part of relative energy deficiency in sport (REDs); management is restoring energy balance (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society 2017).

options.text: Premature ovarian insufficiency
options.rationale: POI requires FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions 4 weeks apart in a woman under 40; her FSH is low and AMH is preserved (Australasian Menopause Society POI).

options.text: Hyperprolactinaemia from a microprolactinoma
options.rationale: Her prolactin is within the normal range (typically <500 mIU/L); a microprolactinoma usually causes prolactin elevations of 1000 to 5000 mIU/L with galactorrhoea (Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology).

options.text: Asherman syndrome
options.rationale: Asherman follows intrauterine instrumentation (D&C, especially postpartum or post-infection) and presents with amenorrhoea despite a normal hormonal axis; she has no such history and her gonadotrophins are clearly suppressed (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Secondary amenorrhoea after pregnancy is excluded is worked up with prolactin, TSH, FSH/LH, oestradiol and androgens. Low FSH/LH/E2 with low energy availability defines functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea (FHA), a component of REDs. PCOS gives elevated LH:FSH and androgens. POI gives FSH >25 IU/L. Hyperprolactinaemia gives elevated prolactin with normal/low FSH/LH. Asherman gives amenorrhoea with a normal hormonal axis after uterine instrumentation.
key_points: FHA = energy deficit, stress, excessive exercise -> suppressed GnRH
REDs replaces female athlete triad and includes male athletes
FHA bone health: low E2 causes accelerated bone loss
Manage FHA by restoring energy balance, not by giving COCP for bone density alone
POI cutoff: FSH >25 IU/L x 2 occasions, age <40
explanation.pearls: Giving the COCP to mask amenorrhoea in FHA does not reliably protect bone and removes the most important marker of recovery; energy restoration is the treatment.

subtopic: Vaginal discharge - Amsel criteria and BV diagnosis
stem: A 26-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of thin grey-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour that is worse after intercourse. She denies vulval itch, dysuria, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, fever, or postcoital bleeding. She is in a new monogamous relationship of 4 months, uses the etonogestrel implant for contraception, and was last screened for STIs 6 months ago with negative results. She is not pregnant. On speculum examination there is a homogeneous thin grey discharge coating the vaginal walls, no cervical motion tenderness, no cervical discharge, and no strawberry cervix. Vaginal pH measured at the lateral vaginal wall is 5.2. A drop of 10% KOH added to a sample of discharge releases a strong amine odour. Wet mount microscopy shows numerous epithelial cells with bacteria adherent to their surface obscuring the cell borders, and no motile flagellated organisms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Thin grey homogeneous discharge. No CMT. Cervix normal.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.2. KOH whiff test positive. Wet mount: clue cells present, no trichomonads, no pseudohyphae.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Vulvovaginal candidiasis
options.rationale: Candidiasis causes thick white curd-like discharge with vulval itch, normal vaginal pH (<4.5), and pseudohyphae or budding yeast on KOH microscopy (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Trichomoniasis
options.rationale: Trichomonas presents with frothy yellow-green discharge, dysuria, vulval irritation, strawberry cervix, and motile flagellated organisms on wet mount; absent here (Australian STI Management Guidelines).

options.text: Chlamydial cervicitis
options.rationale: Chlamydia is often asymptomatic but when symptomatic causes mucopurulent cervical discharge, contact bleeding, and pelvic pain rather than a thin grey homogeneous discharge with a fishy odour; NAAT swab is diagnostic (Australian STI Management Guidelines).

options.text: Atrophic vaginitis
options.rationale: Atrophic vaginitis occurs in oestrogen-deficient women (postmenopausal, lactating, post-oophorectomy) with thin watery discharge, dyspareunia, and pale dry vaginal mucosa, not in a 26-year-old on the implant (Australasian Menopause Society GSM).

options.text: Bacterial vaginosis
options.rationale: Meets all four Amsel criteria (thin homogeneous grey discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells on microscopy) - three of four are sufficient; first-line treatment is oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days or intravaginal metronidazole gel (Australian STI Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: BV is diagnosed when 3 of 4 Amsel criteria are met: thin homogeneous grey-white discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive amine (whiff) test with KOH, and clue cells on saline wet mount. Treatment is oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days or topical metronidazole gel. BV is not classified as an STI but increases the risk of acquiring STIs and is associated with preterm birth in pregnancy.
key_points: Amsel: discharge + pH >4.5 + whiff + clue cells (need 3 of 4)
BV pH characteristically >4.5; candida pH normal <4.5
Clue cells = epithelial cells with adherent coccobacilli obscuring borders
Treat in pregnancy because of preterm birth association
Sexual transmission not established; routine partner treatment not recommended
explanation.pearls: Fishy odour worse after intercourse or menstruation reflects alkaline semen or blood raising vaginal pH and volatilising amines; this is the most specific historical pointer to BV.

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with her fifth episode of vulvovaginal candidiasis in the past 12 months. Each episode features intense vulval pruritus, dyspareunia, thick white curd-like discharge, and erythematous vulval skin, and resolves with a single oral fluconazole 150 mg dose. Between episodes she is asymptomatic. She is in a stable monogamous relationship for 5 years, uses copper IUD for contraception, has no history of diabetes, and a recent HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She does not use scented soaps, wears cotton underwear, and has not used antibiotics in the last 6 months. She has no immunosuppression. Today on speculum examination there is thick white adherent discharge with vulval erythema and excoriation. Vaginal pH is 4.2. KOH microscopy shows numerous pseudohyphae and budding yeast. A high vaginal swab for fungal culture and speciation is collected and confirms Candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, T 36.5 degC, BMI 24.
data_table.Examination: Thick white discharge, vulval erythema and excoriation.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 4.2. KOH: pseudohyphae and budding yeast. HVS culture: Candida albicans, fluconazole sensitive. HbA1c 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing management?

options.text: Single dose fluconazole 150 mg orally and reassure her this is an isolated episode
options.rationale: She has had at least 4 episodes in 12 months, meeting criteria for recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis; isolated single-dose treatment without induction and suppression has a high relapse rate (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Induction with fluconazole 150 mg orally on days 1, 4 and 7, then suppressive fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
options.rationale: Recurrent VVC (>=4 episodes/year) is managed with an induction regimen of fluconazole 150 mg every 72 hours for 3 doses to clear, followed by weekly suppressive fluconazole 150 mg for at least 6 months; this halves recurrence rates (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic; CDC STI Treatment 2021).

options.text: Empirical oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days
options.rationale: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not candidiasis; KOH confirms a fungal infection here (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Refer for an oral glucose tolerance test before any further antifungal therapy
options.rationale: Diabetes is a risk factor for recurrent candidiasis but HbA1c of 5.4% effectively excludes it; an OGTT is not warranted as a precondition for treatment (RACGP Red Book; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Long-term intravaginal boric acid 600 mg nightly indefinitely
options.rationale: Intravaginal boric acid is reserved for non-albicans species (Candida glabrata) or azole-resistant infection; her isolate is fluconazole-sensitive C. albicans (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Recurrent VVC is defined as 4 or more symptomatic episodes per year, confirmed by microscopy or culture. Management combines an induction phase (fluconazole 150 mg orally on days 1, 4 and 7) with suppression (fluconazole 150 mg weekly for at least 6 months). Address modifiable factors: optimise glycaemic control, avoid douching and irritants, and consider non-albicans Candida if treatment-resistant. Liver function should be checked before prolonged azole therapy.
key_points: RVVC: >=4 culture-confirmed episodes per year
Induction: fluconazole 150 mg PO days 1, 4 and 7
Suppression: fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
Test for non-albicans Candida if resistant (glabrata responds to topical boric acid)
Screen for poorly controlled diabetes and consider HIV in immunocompromised picture
explanation.pearls: Routine OGTT is not needed when HbA1c is well below the diabetes threshold; the diagnostic role of HbA1c here is to exclude diabetes as the driver, not to gatekeep treatment.

subtopic: Urinary incontinence - stress vs urge distinction and initial management
stem: A 55-year-old G3P3 presents with 18 months of urinary leakage. She leaks small volumes when she coughs, laughs, sneezes, or lifts her grandchildren, has no urgency, no nocturia, and no leakage on the way to the toilet. She empties her bladder 6 times a day, never wakes overnight to void, and does not feel incomplete emptying. There is no haematuria, no dysuria, no recurrent UTI, and no faecal incontinence. She had three vaginal births with two episiotomies and a forceps delivery for her second child. Her last period was 14 months ago. BMI 31. She smokes 10 cigarettes/day. On examination, abdomen soft, no palpable bladder. On speculum a first-degree anterior wall prolapse is noted. A cough stress test in semi-recumbent and standing positions reproduces leakage of urine from the urethral meatus. Urinalysis is clear and post-void residual on bladder scan is 35 mL.

data_table.Examination: First-degree anterior wall prolapse. Positive cough stress test. No pelvic mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear. PVR 35 mL. Bladder diary not yet completed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer for mid-urethral sling (transobturator tape) procedure
options.rationale: Surgical management is reserved for women who have failed an adequate trial of conservative therapy including supervised pelvic floor muscle training and lifestyle modification; first-line is conservative (RANZCOG; Continence Foundation Australia).

options.text: Commence oral oxybutynin 5 mg bd
options.rationale: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic indicated for urgency urinary incontinence (overactive bladder); the history is pure stress incontinence and antimuscarinics will not help and may cause cognitive and anticholinergic adverse effects (AMH).

options.text: Three-day bladder diary, supervised pelvic floor muscle training for 3 months, weight loss and smoking cessation
options.rationale: First-line for stress urinary incontinence is supervised pelvic floor muscle training for at least 3 months alongside weight loss (5 to 10% reduction halves leakage episodes) and smoking cessation (reduces cough-driven leakage); a bladder diary characterises voiding and is recommended before further intervention (Continence Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert a ring pessary for stress incontinence
options.rationale: A continence pessary or incontinence ring may be useful for stress incontinence but is a second-line conservative option after pelvic floor muscle training; the first-degree prolapse here is not the primary driver (RANZCOG).

options.text: Urodynamic studies before any conservative therapy
options.rationale: Urodynamics are not routinely required before conservative management of uncomplicated stress urinary incontinence; they are indicated when surgery is being considered or when the diagnosis is uncertain (NICE NG123).

explanation.summary: Urinary incontinence is classified as stress (leakage on cough/sneeze/exertion), urge (preceded by urgency, part of overactive bladder), mixed, or overflow (chronic retention with leakage, raised PVR). Initial assessment includes history, bladder diary, urinalysis, PVR, and cough stress test. First-line management for stress and mixed UI is conservative: pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) for at least 3 months, weight loss, smoking cessation, and treatment of constipation. Failure of conservative therapy escalates to mid-urethral sling for stress UI or antimuscarinics/beta-3 agonists for urge UI.
key_points: Stress UI: leak with cough/sneeze, no urgency
Urge UI: leak preceded by urgency, often nocturia and frequency
Overflow UI: raised PVR, chronic retention
PFMT first-line for at least 3 months supervised
Antimuscarinics/mirabegron for urge; sling for stress that fails conservative Rx
explanation.pearls: If you cannot reproduce leakage with a cough stress test and the post-void residual is high, you are not dealing with stress incontinence - rethink the diagnosis before recommending a sling.

subtopic: Pelvic organ prolapse - conservative management of symptomatic stage 2 cystocele
stem: A 68-year-old G4P4 presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge that feels worse by the end of the day and after standing for long periods. She has occasional difficulty initiating micturition and sometimes has to splint the anterior vaginal wall to fully empty. She has no faecal incontinence, no stress urinary incontinence, no dyspareunia (and remains sexually active with her husband), and no postmenopausal bleeding. She is otherwise well, BMI 27, hypertension controlled on perindopril. On examination, speculum reveals descent of the anterior vaginal wall to the level of the hymen on Valsalva (Ba = 0 on POP-Q), with the cervix at -2 and posterior wall at -3. Post-void residual is 80 mL. She is keen to avoid surgery if possible.

data_table.Examination: POP-Q: Ba = 0 (anterior wall at hymen on Valsalva), C = -2, Bp = -3. Stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Anterior colporrhaphy
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for women who have failed or declined conservative management; she is keen to avoid surgery and has not yet trialled physiotherapy or a pessary (RANZCOG Prolapse).

options.text: Sacrocolpopexy with mesh
options.rationale: Sacrocolpopexy is the gold standard surgical repair for apical prolapse, not isolated stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse, and is reserved for surgical candidates after failed conservative management (RANZCOG; TGA mesh statement).

options.text: Vaginal pessary fitting and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy
options.rationale: First-line for symptomatic prolapse in a woman who wishes to avoid surgery is conservative management with a vaginal pessary (ring or Gellhorn depending on support) and supervised pelvic floor muscle training; topical vaginal oestrogen improves pessary tolerance in postmenopausal women (RANZCOG Prolapse; Continence Foundation Australia).

options.text: Insertion of a midurethral sling
options.rationale: Midurethral sling treats stress urinary incontinence, not prolapse; she has no stress urinary incontinence (RANZCOG Urinary Incontinence).

options.text: Indwelling catheter to address the post-void residual
options.rationale: PVR of 80 mL is borderline and may improve with pessary support of the anterior wall; an indwelling catheter is not indicated and would cause harm (Continence Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: POP-Q stages prolapse by the most distal point relative to the hymen: stage 0 no prolapse; stage 1 most distal point >1 cm above hymen; stage 2 within 1 cm of hymen; stage 3 >1 cm beyond hymen but not complete eversion; stage 4 complete eversion. Conservative management (pessary, pelvic floor physiotherapy, topical oestrogen if postmenopausal) is first-line for symptomatic prolapse, particularly in women who wish to avoid surgery or have surgical contraindications. Surgical options include native tissue anterior/posterior repair, sacrocolpopexy for apical defect, and colpocleisis in non-sexually-active women with comorbidities.
key_points: POP-Q stage 2 = leading point within 1 cm of hymen (-1 to +1)
Conservative: pessary + PFMT + topical oestrogen if postmenopausal
Pessary types: ring (mild-moderate), Gellhorn (advanced), cube (refractory)
Sacrocolpopexy = gold standard apical repair
Mesh use restricted in Australia following TGA review; native tissue preferred
explanation.pearls: Stage 2 anterior wall prolapse with urinary symptoms often masks occult stress incontinence; reducing the prolapse with a pessary can unmask leakage that the patient did not previously notice.

subtopic: Chronic pelvic pain - red flag identification and biopsychosocial workup
stem: A 32-year-old G0 presents with 14 months of constant pelvic pain rated 5/10 at baseline and 8/10 cyclically. The pain has both cyclical features (worse in the 4 days prior to and during her period) and non-cyclical features (dull background ache present every day for the last 8 months). She reports dyspareunia on deep penetration, dyschezia during menses, and increased urinary frequency without dysuria or haematuria. She is sexually active in a stable monogamous relationship of 6 years with no STI history, condom use is inconsistent, and last STI screen was 14 months ago and negative. She has been trying to conceive for 9 months without success. There is no fever, no postmenopausal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no unexplained weight loss, no PR bleeding, and no haematuria. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy. On bimanual examination there is uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus with cervical excitation. A pelvic ultrasound 2 months ago showed a 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma with ground-glass appearance and normal uterine cavity. STI screen today is pending. She works as a teacher and reports anxiety and disrupted sleep due to the pain.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC, BMI 23.
data_table.Examination: Uterosacral nodularity. Fixed retroverted uterus. Cervical excitation. No palpable adnexal mass on bimanual.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma. Normal uterine cavity. STI screen pending. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure that endometriosis is confirmed on ultrasound and commence the COCP continuously without further investigation
options.rationale: Although the clinical picture and imaging are consistent with endometriosis, fertility goals, deep endometriosis on examination (uterosacral nodularity, fixed retroversion), and pending STI screen require an integrated specialist assessment; commencing the COCP would suppress fertility in someone trying to conceive (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel IUD now and review in 6 months
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for endometriosis-related pain but is contraceptive and inappropriate for a woman actively trying to conceive; fertility goals must guide therapy (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Start a GnRH agonist with add-back tibolone for 6 months and reassess
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second-line medical therapy reserved for refractory endometriosis after first-line failure; they are contraceptive and would delay fertility (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without imaging or specialist multidisciplinary review
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is no longer required to diagnose endometriosis when imaging and clinical features are characteristic; preoperative MRI for deep disease mapping and multidisciplinary planning improves surgical outcomes and protects fertility (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Refer to a multidisciplinary endometriosis service for specialist assessment, MRI for deep infiltrating disease mapping, and fertility-focused management
options.rationale: Suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral nodularity, dyschezia, fixed retroversion) with an endometrioma and subfertility warrants MRI mapping and multidisciplinary specialist input combining gynaecology, fertility, pelvic pain physiotherapy and psychology; the Australian National Action Plan for Endometriosis recommends this pathway (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; National Action Plan for Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: Chronic pelvic pain workup screens for red flags (postmenopausal bleeding, weight loss, PR bleeding, haematuria, pelvic mass), addresses the bladder, bowel, musculoskeletal, neurological and psychosocial dimensions, and identifies treatable gynaecological pathology (endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID, ovarian cyst). Endometriosis often has clinical and imaging features that are diagnostic without laparoscopy. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral, bowel, bladder, ureteric involvement) requires MRI mapping and multidisciplinary surgical planning. Management is biopsychosocial: medical (NSAID, COCP, progestogen, LNG-IUS, GnRH analogues), surgical (excision/ablation, fertility-preserving), pain-focused (CBT, physiotherapy, mindfulness), and fertility-specific (IVF if needed).
key_points: CPP red flags: PMB, weight loss, PR bleeding, haematuria, fever, mass
Endometriosis no longer requires laparoscopy for diagnosis if imaging/clinical features are characteristic
Deep endometriosis suspected: MRI mapping + multidisciplinary review
LNG-IUS, COCP, progestogens are first-line medical Rx (contraceptive)
Fertility goals dictate therapy choice; consider IVF early in deep disease
explanation.pearls: Uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus on bimanual examination are physical signs of deep infiltrating endometriosis - imaging may underestimate disease, so multidisciplinary review precedes any surgical decision.

stem: A 29-year-old G1P1 presents for preconception counselling. Her first child was born 18 months ago at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal birth weighing 3450 g. She takes sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for generalised tonic-clonic epilepsy (last seizure 4 years ago) and is co-managed by neurology. She is otherwise well. She has no personal or family history of neural tube defects, no diabetes, no malabsorption, and a BMI of 24. She is up to date with her cervical screening (HPV negative 8 months ago) and her booking bloods from her last pregnancy showed she was rubella immune and varicella immune. She smokes 5 cigarettes a day, drinks 4 standard drinks twice a week on weekends, and does not use any other supplements. She would like to start trying for a second child in 3 months and asks what she should do to prepare.

data_table.Investigations: Rubella IgG immune (last pregnancy). Varicella IgG immune. Cervical screen HPV negative 8 months ago. HbA1c 5.2% (33 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most important pre-conception intervention?

options.text: Folic acid 0.5 mg daily for 3 months pre-conception and the first trimester
options.rationale: Standard 0.5 mg daily is recommended for low-risk women; sodium valproate is a major teratogen (neural tube defects, cardiac, cleft, autism, developmental delay) and elevates risk to a level requiring 5 mg daily (RANZCOG Pre-pregnancy Counselling; NHMRC).

options.text: Iodine 150 microg daily and vitamin D testing only
options.rationale: Iodine 150 microg/day is recommended in pre-pregnancy but does not address the dominant teratogenic risk from sodium valproate; folate stratification and medication review are more important interventions (RANZCOG; NHMRC).

options.text: Reassure that she does not need any change to therapy as she is seizure-free
options.rationale: Seizure control does not mitigate the teratogenic risk of valproate; the drug itself is the harm, irrespective of whether seizures are controlled (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: MMR and varicella booster immunisation now
options.rationale: She is already immune to rubella and varicella on serology; live vaccines are not indicated and are contraindicated within 28 days of attempting conception in any case (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Refer to neurology to discuss switching to a safer anti-seizure medication and prescribe folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 1 month pre-conception until 12 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Sodium valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk of any anti-seizure medication (10% major congenital malformation rate, neurodevelopmental effects in 30 to 40%); switching to a safer agent (e.g. lamotrigine or levetiracetam) before conception is mandatory and high-dose folate 5 mg is indicated because of the elevated neural tube defect risk (RANZCOG; NHMRC; AMH).

explanation.summary: Pre-conception counselling covers folate (0.5 mg/day low-risk; 5 mg/day high-risk: previous NTD, diabetes, anti-epileptic drugs, malabsorption, family history NTD, obesity BMI >30 in some guidelines), iodine 150 microg/day, rubella and varicella serology with vaccination if non-immune (4 weeks before conception), cervical screening, STI screening, optimisation of chronic disease (diabetes, hypertension, thyroid, epilepsy), medication review for teratogens (valproate, ACE inhibitors, warfarin, retinoids, methotrexate), smoking and alcohol cessation, weight optimisation, mental health, and partner factors.
key_points: Folate low-risk 0.5 mg; high-risk 5 mg from 1 to 3 months pre-conception through 12 weeks
Iodine 150 microg/day pre-conception and throughout pregnancy and lactation
Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) at least 4 weeks before conception attempts
Sodium valproate is the most teratogenic AED; switch pre-conception
Other teratogens: ACEi/ARB, warfarin, isotretinoin, methotrexate, mycophenolate
explanation.pearls: If a woman of reproductive age is taking sodium valproate, the bigger intervention is changing the drug, not adding folate; high-dose folate is mitigation, not protection.

subtopic: Recurrent pregnancy loss - antiphospholipid syndrome workup
stem: A 33-year-old G3P0 presents after her third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. The first was a missed miscarriage at 9 weeks managed expectantly, the second was a complete miscarriage at 8 weeks managed at home, and the third was a missed miscarriage at 10+2 managed with misoprostol 4 weeks ago. She conceives easily within 1 to 2 cycles each time and has regular 28-day ovulatory cycles. She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, deep vein thrombosis, stroke, or pulmonary embolism, no symptoms of systemic lupus erythematosus (no malar rash, no arthralgia, no oral ulcers, no Raynaud), no autoimmune disease, no fertility treatment, and no live births. She has never smoked, drinks one standard drink per week, has a BMI of 27, and is otherwise well. Her partner is a 35-year-old non-smoker with no children from previous relationships. There is no family history of recurrent miscarriage, balanced translocation, congenital malformation, or genetic disease, and no consanguinity. She is taking folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Examination is unremarkable. A transvaginal ultrasound shows a normal uterine cavity, both ovaries normal, and no fibroids; antral follicle count is normal. Karyotype of products of conception from the third miscarriage was 46,XX (euploid). TSH is 1.8 mIU/L and HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She asks what should be tested before her next pregnancy.

data_table.Investigations: TVUS: normal uterine cavity, no fibroids, both ovaries normal. POC karyotype third miscarriage 46,XX (euploid). TSH 1.8 mIU/L. HbA1c 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most important next investigation?

options.text: Parental karyotype testing as the first investigation
options.rationale: Parental karyotype is not routinely first-line; it is reserved for couples with a euploid first-trimester loss combined with additional features (advanced maternal age, family history) or when products show structural rearrangement. Yield of parental balanced translocation is approximately 2 to 5% (ESHRE RPL 2023; RANZCOG).

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen (factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, protein C, protein S, antithrombin)
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in recurrent miscarriage outside of research settings because results do not change management of pregnancy loss; treatment with anticoagulation in inherited thrombophilia alone does not improve live birth rates (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Hysteroscopy regardless of ultrasound findings
options.rationale: Saline infusion sonography or hysteroscopy is reserved for cases where ultrasound is equivocal or suggests cavity abnormality; her TVUS shows a normal cavity (ESHRE).

options.text: Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin antibodies, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies on two occasions 12 weeks apart
options.rationale: After 3 consecutive miscarriages (or 2 by RANZCOG criteria), antiphospholipid syndrome screening is the highest yield test that has direct therapeutic implications (aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH in next pregnancy if positive); diagnosis requires laboratory criteria on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart (RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage; ESHRE RPL 2023).

options.text: Natural killer cell assay
options.rationale: Peripheral or uterine NK cell testing is not validated for recurrent miscarriage and is explicitly not recommended by ESHRE and RANZCOG; it does not change management and adds unjustified expense (ESHRE RPL 2023).

explanation.summary: Recurrent pregnancy loss is defined by RANZCOG as 2 or more consecutive miscarriages and by some international bodies as 3 or more. Workup with evidence-based yield includes APS screen (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2-GP-I on two occasions 12 weeks apart), thyroid function and TPO antibodies, HbA1c, parental karyotype if products show structural abnormality, and pelvic imaging to exclude uterine anomaly. APS is the only treatable cause with clear therapeutic benefit: low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH improves live birth rates.
key_points: RANZCOG RPL definition: >=2 consecutive miscarriages
APS labs: LA, aCL, anti-beta-2-GP-I, repeated 12 weeks apart
APS treatment: aspirin 100 mg + prophylactic LMWH in pregnancy
Thyroid (TSH, TPO) and HbA1c also worth screening
Inherited thrombophilia and NK cell tests are NOT recommended
explanation.pearls: Half of all recurrent miscarriages remain unexplained even after thorough workup; the most important counselling point is that an unexplained workup still predicts a 60 to 75% chance of subsequent live birth.

subtopic: Infertility workup in a couple under 35
stem: A 30-year-old G0 and her 32-year-old male partner present after 13 months of trying to conceive with regular unprotected intercourse 2 to 3 times per week, including timed intercourse around predicted ovulation in the last 4 cycles. She has regular 29-day cycles with predictable ovulatory symptoms (mid-cycle mucus change and mittelschmerz). She has no dysmenorrhoea, no dyspareunia, no pelvic surgery, no PID, no STI history, and was up to date with cervical screening 6 months ago (HPV negative). BMI 24, non-smoker, no alcohol, no caffeine excess. She takes folic acid 0.5 mg daily and iodine 150 microg daily. Her partner is well, BMI 26, non-smoker, no known fertility issues, no previous children, no varicocele symptoms, no testicular trauma, no mumps orchitis, no inguinal surgery, no cryptorchidism, no chronic illness, no anabolic steroid use, takes no medications, drinks 6 standard drinks per week, and his work as an electrician does not involve heat exposure. There is no family history of infertility, premature menopause, or genetic disease on either side. On her examination there are no abnormal findings; bimanual is normal with no nodularity. She is concerned and asks what investigations should be done now.

data_table.Investigations: Cervical screen HPV negative 6 months ago. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial set of investigations?

options.text: Semen analysis, mid-luteal (day 21) progesterone, TSH, transvaginal ultrasound; reserve HSG for after the initial workup
options.rationale: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35 (6 months if 35 or over). First-line investigations are semen analysis (assesses 40 to 50% of contributory causes), confirmation of ovulation (mid-luteal progesterone), thyroid function, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HSG or HyCoSy) is performed after initial workup if no abnormality is found (RANZCOG Infertility; NICE CG156).

options.text: Refer immediately for IVF without any further investigation
options.rationale: IVF is indicated after a defined cause is identified or following empirical workup; jumping to IVF without identifying treatable causes is inappropriate and ignores potential male factor (RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue trying for another 12 months before any investigation
options.rationale: She is over 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse and meets the definition of infertility; delaying investigation reduces her chances of timely treatment (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Hysterosalpingogram and laparoscopy as the first-line investigations
options.rationale: Tubal patency assessment is part of the workup but follows initial investigations (semen analysis, ovulation confirmation, ultrasound); laparoscopy as first-line is invasive and unjustified (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: AMH for ovarian reserve only and reassure she can wait given young age
options.rationale: AMH is part of the broader workup but does not predict spontaneous conception; reassurance without male factor or tubal workup misses 50% of contributory causes (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35, or 6 months at age 35 or older, or earlier if there is a known cause. Initial workup is parallel investigation of both partners: semen analysis (according to WHO criteria), ovulation assessment (mid-luteal progesterone, cycle history, ultrasound follicle tracking), ovarian reserve markers (AMH, antral follicle count, FSH on day 2 to 5), thyroid, prolactin, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HyCoSy or HSG) follows. Laparoscopy is reserved for suspected endometriosis or adhesive disease.
key_points: <35: investigate after 12 months; >=35: investigate after 6 months
Male factor accounts for 40 to 50% - always do semen analysis
Mid-luteal (day 21 in 28-day cycle) progesterone >25 nmol/L = ovulation
Tubal patency: HyCoSy preferred (no radiation) or HSG
AMH for ovarian reserve, not as a predictor of spontaneous conception
explanation.pearls: The most common omission in infertility workup is the semen analysis - both partners must be investigated in parallel from the outset.

subtopic: Adolescent endometriosis - high index of suspicion and empirical management
stem: A 15-year-old presents with her mother because of worsening period pain and bleeding. Menarche was at 12; cycles are regular at 28 days but have become increasingly painful since age 14, now causing her to miss 3 to 5 school days per month. Pain begins 24 hours before bleeding, peaks on day 2, and is associated with nausea, vomiting, and inability to get out of bed. She soaks through a fully saturated super pad every 90 minutes on days 1 and 2 and reports passing 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She has tried paracetamol 1 g qid and ibuprofen 400 mg tds with only partial relief. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy at age 22 and a paternal aunt has known endometriosis. There is no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no dyschezia, no haematuria, no fever, and no postcoital bleeding. She has no chronic disease. She is no longer attending netball training and has begun to refuse social outings and sleepovers during her periods, and her mother reports she has been increasingly tearful and withdrawn in the days leading up to bleeding. On abdominal examination there is mild suprapubic tenderness; she declines an internal examination. A pregnancy test is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.5 degC, BMI 22.
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness. Declines internal examination.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. FBC: Hb 116 g/L, MCV 82 fL, ferritin 18 microg/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial scheduled NSAID (naproxen 500 mg bd from day -1 to day 3) plus continuous COCP and a transabdominal pelvic ultrasound; refer to a paediatric gynaecologist if not improved at 3 months
options.rationale: Adolescent dysmenorrhoea with severe functional impact, heavy bleeding, family history of endometriosis, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia warrants empirical scheduled NSAID plus continuous (cycle-suppressing) COCP, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal not appropriate in a non-sexually-active adolescent), and specialist referral if no improvement at 3 months; diagnosis does not require laparoscopy in adolescents (RANZCOG Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; ESHRE).

options.text: Reassure that severe period pain in adolescence is normal and continue current analgesia
options.rationale: Missing 3 to 5 school days per month, social withdrawal, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia is not normal and reflects a high probability of endometriosis or other secondary cause; dismissing severe dysmenorrhoea delays diagnosis by an average of 7 to 12 years (Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is not first-line in adolescents; empirical medical therapy is trialled first and laparoscopy reserved for those who fail medical management or have suspected deep disease (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Commence a GnRH agonist with hormone add-back
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory endometriosis after failure of first-line hormonal therapy; long-term use in adolescents carries bone density concerns and is not first-line (ESHRE).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system without prior medical trial
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for adolescent endometriosis and heavy menstrual bleeding but is generally a second-line option after COCP trial in a non-sexually-active adolescent; insertion may require general anaesthetic (Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Adolescent endometriosis is under-recognised; diagnostic delay averages 7 to 12 years. School avoidance, heavy menstrual bleeding, severe dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to first-line analgesia, and a family history are red flags. Empirical management combines scheduled NSAID, continuous (suppressive) COCP to eliminate menstruation, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound, and early specialist referral. Transvaginal ultrasound is generally not appropriate in non-sexually-active adolescents. LNG-IUS is a second-line option and may require GA for insertion. GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory disease and require add-back therapy. Laparoscopy is not required to diagnose endometriosis in adolescents when the clinical picture is consistent.
key_points: Diagnostic delay in endometriosis averages 7 to 12 years
Adolescent red flags: school absence, family hx, refractory to first-line
Empirical Rx: scheduled NSAID + continuous COCP; TA ultrasound
TVUS inappropriate in non-sexually-active adolescent
LNG-IUS effective but second-line in adolescents; consider GA for insertion
explanation.pearls: If a 15-year-old is missing school every month for period pain, do not normalise it - empirical treatment plus follow-up is the standard of care, and the family history doubles the prior probability of endometriosis.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with 4 months of intermittent postcoital bleeding. Bleeding is small-volume, bright red, and occurs after most episodes of intercourse but not between periods. She has regular 28-day cycles with normal flow and no dysmenorrhoea. She is on the combined oral contraceptive pill, in a stable relationship of 18 months, and her last cervical screening test was 12 months ago and was HPV negative. She has no pelvic pain, no abnormal discharge, and no dyspareunia. She has had two previous male sexual partners and never had a sexually transmitted infection.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Investigations: Last HPV test 12 months ago: HPV negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Postcoital bleeding warrants prompt evaluation - it can reflect cervicitis, polyp, ectropion, or cancer; deferring evaluation without examination is inappropriate (NCSP).

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test now
options.rationale: An HPV-negative test 12 months ago has a high negative predictive value for high-grade lesions; the symptomatic pathway here is speculum examination and STI workup, not repeat screening (NCSP).

options.text: Refer directly to colposcopy without speculum examination
options.rationale: Colposcopy is indicated for suspicious cervical lesions found on examination or for positive screening results; direct referral without prior speculum and STI screen skips first-line assessment (NCSP).

options.text: Stop the combined oral contraceptive pill as the cause
options.rationale: COCP-related ectropion can cause postcoital bleeding but ectropion is identified on speculum examination, not by empirically stopping the pill before assessment (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Speculum examination with assessment of the cervix and chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT swabs
options.rationale: Postcoital bleeding requires direct visualisation of the cervix (to identify ectropion, polyp, cervicitis, or suspicious lesion) and an STI workup including chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT, which are common causes of cervicitis-related contact bleeding; her recent negative HPV test makes high-grade cervical cancer less likely but does not exclude polyp or cervicitis (Australian STI Management Guidelines; NCSP).

explanation.summary: Postcoital bleeding in a woman of reproductive age has a differential of cervical ectropion (common in COCP users and pregnancy), cervicitis (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, Mycoplasma genitalium, trichomonas), endocervical polyp, atrophic changes, and rarely cervical or vaginal cancer. Workup is speculum examination, cervical screening if not up to date, STI NAAT swabs (endocervical or self-collected vaginal), and colposcopy if a suspicious lesion is identified on inspection. A recent negative HPV test substantially reduces but does not exclude cancer.
key_points: PCB differential: ectropion, cervicitis, polyp, atrophy, cancer
Speculum + chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT is first-line
Colposcopy if visible suspicious lesion or screen positive
Recent HPV-negative test does not rule out polyp or cervicitis
Ectropion = columnar epithelium on ectocervix, often physiological in COCP users
explanation.pearls: Cancer is a rare cause of postcoital bleeding in women under 35 with recent negative HPV screening, but speculum examination remains mandatory - imaging cannot replace direct visual inspection of the cervix.

subtopic: Ovarian torsion in an adolescent - urgent surgical management
stem: A 14-year-old presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of sudden severe left iliac fossa pain. The pain began abruptly while she was playing netball, is sharp, constant, radiates to the left flank, and has been associated with vomiting four times in the last 2 hours. She had two previous brief episodes of less severe left-sided pain in the last 6 months, each lasting 30 minutes and resolving spontaneously. Menarche was at 13; her last menstrual period was 12 days ago and was normal. She has never been sexually active. There is no fever, no urinary symptoms, no diarrhoea, no PR bleeding, no recent trauma, and no chronic illness. On examination she is in obvious distress, BP 110/68, HR 116, T 37.0, with marked left iliac fossa tenderness and guarding, and a tender palpable mass. Abdomen is otherwise soft. Beta-hCG is negative. White cell count is 13.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 18 mg/L. A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound with Doppler shows a 7 cm complex left adnexal mass with peripherally displaced follicles, a hyperechoic whirlpool sign at the suspected pedicle, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow within the left ovary. The right ovary is normal. There is no free fluid. She has fasted for 5 hours, has IV access in the right antecubital fossa, and morphine 5 mg IV has been given with partial relief. Her parents have given consent for emergency anaesthesia and laparoscopy. The gynaecology registrar and on-call anaesthetist have reviewed her and the operating theatre is available within 30 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, T 37.0 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Marked left iliac fossa tenderness with guarding. Tender left adnexal mass palpable.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. WCC 13.4 x 10^9/L. CRP 18 mg/L. TVUS Doppler: 7 cm complex left adnexal mass, peripherally displaced follicles, whirlpool sign, absent venous flow, reduced arterial flow. Right ovary normal. No free fluid.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia and repeat the ultrasound in 12 hours
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency; every hour of delay increases ovarian necrosis and reduces salvage rate. Observation while imaging declares itself is harmful (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to confirm the diagnosis before surgical decision
options.rationale: MRI adds delay without improving on Doppler ultrasound findings that are already characteristic; the diagnosis is a clinical and ultrasound diagnosis warranting immediate surgery (RANZCOG).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy for detorsion and ovarian preservation, with oophoropexy considered intraoperatively
options.rationale: Whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles and absent venous flow on Doppler in an adolescent with characteristic clinical features is ovarian torsion. The standard of care is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion regardless of macroscopic appearance (ovaries that look black often recover function); oophorectomy is reserved for necrotic non-viable tissue or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence (RANZCOG Ovarian Cysts; ESHRE; Royal Women's Hospital Clinical Guidelines).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy with planned left salpingo-oophorectomy because the ovary appears non-viable on Doppler
options.rationale: Doppler signs do not reliably predict viability; even ovaries that appear black at surgery have a high rate of functional recovery after detorsion. Oophorectomy in an adolescent should be reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and admit for suspected pelvic inflammatory disease
options.rationale: She has never been sexually active and the imaging is diagnostic of torsion, not tubo-ovarian abscess; antibiotics do not treat torsion and delay surgery (Australian STI Management Guidelines; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Clinical features are sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, often with vomiting, intermittent prior episodes, and a palpable adnexal mass. Doppler ultrasound findings of whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow support the diagnosis, but normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion (dual ovarian blood supply can preserve arterial flow). Management is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian preservation; oophorectomy is reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence, particularly when no ovarian mass is found or in cases with redundant ligaments. Outcomes correlate with time to surgery.
key_points: Sudden severe unilateral pain + vomiting + adnexal mass = torsion until excluded
Doppler whirlpool sign, peripheral follicles, absent venous flow = highly suggestive
Normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion (dual blood supply)
Detorsion + ovarian preservation is the standard, even if the ovary looks ischaemic
Oophoropexy reduces recurrence, especially when no mass is present
explanation.pearls: Black does not equal dead - detorsed ischaemic-looking ovaries regularly recover function in adolescents, and the decision to remove an ovary in a 14-year-old should be reserved for clear necrosis or proven malignancy.
